Name: gpu_optimized_sparse_attention
Title: GPU-Optimized Sparse Attention for Efficient Large-Scale Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging sparsity in the attention mechanism of large-scale transformer models and optimizing the corresponding GPU kernels, we can significantly reduce inference latency and memory usage without compromising model accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works have explored various optimizations for transformer models, including kernel-level optimizations (FlashRNN, DF-GNN), dynamic kernel selection (DKS), and block low-rank (BLR) compression (Memory-Efficient Acceleration of Block Low-Rank Foundation Models). However, these works primarily focus on dense operations or specific hardware configurations. Sparse attention mechanisms have been shown to reduce computational and memory costs, but they often lack hardware-optimized implementations. This proposal aims to bridge this gap by developing and optimizing sparse attention kernels for GPUs, ensuring they are both efficient and scalable.
Abstract: Transformer models, despite their success in various tasks, suffer from high computational and memory demands, especially during inference. Sparse attention mechanisms have been proposed to mitigate these issues, but existing implementations often lack hardware-optimized kernels, leading to suboptimal performance. We propose GPU-Optimized Sparse Attention (GOSA), a framework that combines sparsity in the attention mechanism with highly optimized GPU kernels. GOSA introduces custom Triton kernels for sparse attention, leveraging techniques such as block sparsity, partial fusion, and memory layout optimizations. We evaluate GOSA on a range of transformer models, including BERT and GPT, and demonstrate significant reductions in inference latency and memory usage while maintaining or even improving model accuracy. Our experiments show that GOSA can achieve up to 3x speedups and 2x memory reductions compared to dense attention baselines on NVIDIA GPUs.

Experiments.Description: Benchmark the performance of the dense attention mechanism using existing PyTorch and cuDNN implementations.
Experiments.Metrics: Latency, memory usage, throughput, and accuracy on BERT and GPT models.
Experiments.Method: Run inference on a variety of sequence lengths and batch sizes, measuring the time and memory usage for each configuration.

Experiments.Description: Implement custom Triton kernels for sparse attention, focusing on block sparsity and partial fusion.
Experiments.Metrics: Kernel performance, including latency, memory usage, and throughput.
Experiments.Method: Profile the kernels using nvprof and Nsight, focusing on warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and occupancy. Optimize the kernels iteratively based on the profiling results.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the performance of the sparse attention kernels on the same transformer models and configurations as the dense baseline.
Experiments.Metrics: Latency, memory usage, throughput, and accuracy.
Experiments.Method: Compare the results of the sparse attention implementation with the dense baseline, ensuring the same model accuracy is maintained or improved.

Experiments.Description: Conduct A/B latency races under fixed accuracy budgets to quantify the performance gains.
Experiments.Metrics: p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
Experiments.Method: Run multiple inference trials on the same hardware with different configurations, measuring the latency and throughput for each trial. Report the results with variance across runs.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The sparsity patterns in attention mechanisms may vary across different models and tasks, requiring careful tuning and validation.
Optimizing sparse kernels for different GPU architectures and driver versions may introduce additional complexity and overhead.
There is a risk that the optimized sparse kernels may not generalize well to all transformer models, especially those with unique architectural designs.
